The working committee, the ruling body of the Congress, is not elected, but nominated by the president; the election of the president is thus a constitutional opportunity through which the membership expressed the nature of the leadership of the Congress. With Bose’s victory the polarisation in terms of ideology and method of future struggle was clear. Thus the election of Bose, in the face of the opposition of the official machine, led to a sharp inner crisis. Subhash Bose had accused the working committee leaders of being ready to reach a compromise with the government on the matter of federation. Now, those leaders felt they could not work with a president who had publicly cast doubts on their nationalistic principles and resigned from the working committee. Nationalist Response in the Wake of World War II ✫✫✫✫✫ 419 Bose was ill when the Tripuri session took place, but he attended it and in his presidential speech he prophecised that an imperialist war was about to take place in Europe. He declared: “In the first place, we must give clear and unequivocal expression to what I have been feeling for some time past, namely, that the time has come for us to raise the issue of Swaraj and submit our national demand to the British government in the form of an ultimatum...” He was in favour of giving a six-month ultimatum to Britain to grant the national demand of independence; if the ultimatum was rejected, he said, a mass civil disobedience movement should be launched. In his opinion, as Bose was to write later, the Congress was strong enough just as the masses were ready for such a struggle. He felt that advantage should be taken of the international crisis to strive for independence. Gandhi, on the other hand, was firm in the belief that it was not the time for such ultimatums as neither the Congress nor the masses were yet ready for struggle. He was also aware that there were communal discord and class strife and a lack of unified vision and that this would undermine any movement. A resolution was moved by Govind Ballabh Pant, reaffirming faith in Gandhian policies and asking Bose to nominate the working committee “in accordance with the wishes of Gandhiji”, and it was passed without opposition from the socialists or the communists. Apparently, the Left was not keen on discarding Gandhi’s leadership. However, Gandhi said that he would not like to impose a working committee on the president and that, since Bose was the president, he should choose the members of the working committee and lead the Congress. Bose continued his effort to win Gandhi’s confidence but did not succeed. Bose refused to nominate a new working committee. Bose wanted an immediate struggle led by Gandhi, whereas Gandhi was firm in his belief that the time was not ripe for struggle. The problem was that ideologically Gandhi and Bose were on different platforms. Gandhi was not willing to lead a Congress struggle based on the radical lines preferred by Bose, even as Bose was not willing to 420 ✫✫✫✫✫ A Brief History of Modern India compromise on his ideas. Gandhi was of the view that he would either lead the Congress on the basis of his own strategy and style of politics or surrender the position of the leader. In his reply to a letter from Bose, Gandhi wrote: “The views you express seem to be so diametrically opposed to those of the others and my own that I do not see any possibility of bridging them.” Bose had misjudged the support he had got in his election. Even the socialists and the communists for the most part were not keen on a split in the Congress. They realised that a split would reduce the Left (which was not very consolidated at the time) to a splinter group. They preferred a united Congress led by Gandhi, as the national struggle was of utmost importance and the Congress was at the time the main organ of this struggle. In the circumstances, Bose saw no option but to resign. He resigned from the president’s post in April 1939. This led to the election of Rajendra Prasad as president of the Congress. The crisis in the Congress had been overcome for the present. In May, Bose and his followers formed the Forward Bloc (at Makur, Unnao) as a new party within the Congress. But when he gave a call for an all-India protest on July 9 against an AICC resolution, the Congress Working Committee took disciplinary action against Bose: in August 1939, he was removed from the post of president of the Bengal Provincial Congress Committee besides being debarred from holding any elective office in the Congress for a period of three years. Among the resolutions at Tripuri was an interesting one relating to China: “The Congress sends its greetings to the people of China and its deepest sympathy in their trials and privations in their struggle against ruthless and inhuman imperialism. It congratulates them on their heroic resistance. “The Congress expresses its approval of the sending of a Medical Mission on its behalf to the people of China and trusts that this Mission will continue to receive full support, so that it may carry on its work of succour effectively and be a worthy symbol of Indian solidarity with China.” Nationalist Response in the Wake of World War II ✫✫✫✫✫ 421 Gandhi and Bose: Ideological Differences Gandhi and Subhash Bose had a deep respect for one another despite their hugely differing ideologies. Each appreciated the work done by the other in the national struggle for freedom. In 1942, Gandhi called Bose the “Prince among the Patriots”. When the death of Bose was reported, Gandhi said that Netaji’s “patriotism is second to none... His bravery shines through all his actions. He aimed high and failed. But who has not failed.” On another occasion Gandhi said, “Netaji will remain immortal for all time to come for his service to India.” Bose was fully aware of Gandhi’s importance as a symbol of Indian nationalism and called him “The Father of Our Nation” in a radio broadcast from Rangoon in 1944 even though in the same speech he expressed his own conviction that force was the only way to win freedom from the British. When forced to resign at the Tripuri session, Bose said he would “yield to none in my respect for his (Gandhi’s) personality”, adding that “it will be a tragic thing for me if I succeed in winning the confidence of other people but fail to win the confidence of India’s greatest man.” Later, Bose said that the “service which Mahatma Gandhi has rendered to India and to the cause of India’s freedom is so unique and unparalleled that his name will be written in letters of gold in our National History—for all time”. Incidentally, both men considered socialism to be the way forward in India, though in slightly different ways. Gandhi did not subscribe to the Western form of socialism which he associated with industrialisation, but agreed with the kind of socialism advocated by Jayaprakash Narayan. Both Gandhi and Bose were religious men and disliked communism. Both worked against untouchability and spoke for women’s emancipation.

But they differed widely in their ways and methods and in their political and economic ideologies. Non-Violence versus Militant Approach Gandhi was a firm believer in ahimsa and satyagraha, the non- violent way to gain any goal. He believed that it was the way 422 ✫✫✫✫✫ A Brief History of Modern India in which the masses could be involved. He objected to violence firstly because an unarmed masses had little chance of success in an armed rebellion, and then because he considered violence a clumsy weapon which created more problems than it solved, and left behind hatred and bitterness which could not be overcome through reconciliation. Bose believed that Gandhi’s strategy based on the ideology of non-violence would be inadequate for securing India’s independence. To his mind, violent resistance alone could oust the alien imperialist rule from India. He considered the Gandhian civil disobedience campaign as an effective means of paralysing the administration, but did not think it to be efficacious unless accompanied by a movement aimed at total revolution that was prepared, if necessary, to use violence. Means and Ends Bose had his eye on the result of the action. When war clouds hung over Europe, he saw the situation as an opportunity to take advantage of the British weakness. He believed in seizing whatever opportunity was available to carry forward the struggle for freedom. He openly criticised the British for professing to fight for the freedom of the European nations under Nazi control but refusing to grant independence to its own colonies, including India. He had no compunction in taking the help of the Nazis or the Fascists and later of Imperial Japan—the ‘Axis powers’ as they came to be called when the war broke out—even though he believed in freedom and equality and other liberal ideals and disapproved of the arrogant racialism of the Nazis and the suppression of democratic institutions in Nazi Germany (as his writings show). However, he admired the Nazis and the Fascists for their discipline. Bose’s supporters point out that his association with Germany and Japan was dictated by revolutionary strategy and not by ideological kinship. In other words, he was just a pragmatist; he was against the Fascist theory of racial superiority and the Fascist acceptance of capitalism. Gandhi felt that the non-violent way of protest that he propagated could not be practised unless the means and ends Nationalist Response in the Wake of World War II ✫✫✫✫✫ 423 were equally good. One could not just use any means to achieve an end however desirable that end may be. It would be against the truth that should guide one in all actions. Besides, he had a deep dislike for the ideas of the Fascists and the Nazis and would not think of using them to ally against the British, especially when the latter were in a difficult situation. He saw Nazi Germany and Imperial Japan not just as aggressors but as dangerous powers. Gandhi himself said: “The difference of outlook between him (Bose) and me as to the means is too well known for comment.” Bose acknowledged that Gandhi’s methods had their importance when he said in his speech from Tokyo: “Though personally I believe that this method will not succeed in bringing us complete independence, there is no doubt that it has greatly helped to rouse and unify the Indian people and also to keep up a movement of resistance against the foreign government.” Form of Government In his early writings, Bose expressed the opinion that democracy was the acceptable political system for India. But later, he seemed to have veered towards the idea that, at least in the beginning, a democratic system would not be adequate for the process of nation rebuilding and the eradication of poverty and social inequality. In an address to students in Tokyo University in 1944, Bose is quoted as saying: “You cannot have a so-called democratic system, if that system has to put through economic reforms on a socialistic basis. Therefore we must have a political system—a State—of an authoritarian character....” [When Bose proclaimed, on October 21, 1943, the formation of the Provisional Government of Azad Hind (Free India), he held on to his post as Supreme Commander of the Indian National Army, and also named himself head of state, prime minister, and minister for war and foreign affairs. He anticipated retaining the position of head of state in a free India. This, say some scholars, indicated the authoritative streak in Bose.] As early as 1930, Bose expressed the opinion that in India there should be “a synthesis of what modern Europe 424 ✫✫✫✫✫ A Brief History of Modern India calls Socialism and Fascism. We have here the justice, the equality, the love, which is the basis of Socialism, and combined with that we have the efficiency and the discipline of Fascism as it stands in Europe today.” He called this ‘samyavada ’. Bose admired discipline and orderly approach to anything. He admired these qualities in the Fascists of Italy and in the Nazis of Germany. Indeed, it is clear from his letters that, despite his dislike of colonial power and his desire to oust the alien British rule from India, he was impressed by the methodical and systematic approach of the British and their disciplined way of life. Bose, however, was not a Nazi or a Fascist, for he supported empowerment of women, secularism and other liberal ideas. Neither was Bose a communist: he considered himself “a socialist, but that was a very different thing from being a communist”. He laughed at the idea of internationalism as espoused by the communists; he said nationalism was important before going on to internationalism. He also felt that the theoretical ideals found in Marx’s writings could not be applied in India without a lot of modification. Nor did he discard religion which was important to him. Bose was a leftist in the sense that he was an anti-imperialist and believed in attaining complete independence. After the achievement of independence, Bose considered leftism would mean socialism; the reconstruction of national life would have to be on a socialist basis. Indeed, it would appear from many of his writings that, after an initial stage of authoritarian rule, there could be formed “a new India and a happy India on the basis of the eternal principles of liberty, democracy and socialism.” Gandhi’s ideas on government can be found in the Hind Swaraj (1909); it was “the nearest he came to producing a sustained work of political theory.” Gandhi’s idealised state, his Ramrajya —a utopia, in fact—did not need a representative government, a constitution, an army or a police force. Capitalism, communism, exploitation and religious violence would be absent. Instead, the country was to be modelled on the India of the past. In many ways, Gandhi’s writings call for a pre-modern, morally-enlightened and apolitical Indian state. Swaraj lays stress on self-governance through individuals Nationalist Response in the Wake of World War II ✫✫✫✫✫ 425 and community building. “At the individual level Swaraj is vitally connected with the capacity for dispassionate self- assessment, ceaseless self-purification and growing self- reliance.” Gandhi said: “I look upon an increase in the power of the state with greatest fear, because although while apparently doing good by minimising exploitation, it does the greatest harm to mankind by destroying individuality which is at the root of progress”. He was sceptical of the party system and sure that representative democracy could not provide people with justice. He advocated a stateless society in which life becomes perfect. Gandhi was opposed to centralisation. He believed in decentralisation of political as well as economic power, and this could come about only by beginning from the basic unit. In his vision of swaraj, society would be composed of “innumerable… ever-widening, never-ascending” village republics. The basic unit would be the village whose people will always abide by the ideals of truth and non-violence. Every village would be a self-sufficient republic or panchayat. (Self-sufficiency did not mean that in times of need help could not be taken from other villages.) The panchayat, the unit of local self-government, will consist of five persons – male and female – elected annually. It would represent the village community and be the custodian of all authority. Gandhi and Subhash Chandra Bose differed fundamentally in their ideological outlooks, especially on the means to achieve independence, the nature of the state, economic policy, and social reforms.  

**Means of struggle** – Gandhi’s strategy was rooted in *ahimsa* (non‑violence) and *satyagraha* (truth‑based civil disobedience). He believed that an unarmed mass could succeed only through moral force and that violence would breed hatred and bitterness. Bose, by contrast, viewed violent resistance as essential; he argued that the British could be ousted only by a “total revolution” that might employ force, and he was willing to ally with the Axis powers to exploit the wartime weakness of Britain.  

**Government and political system** – Gandhi envisioned a stateless, decentralized society of self‑sufficient village republics (panchayats) governed by *swaraj* (self‑rule). He opposed representative democracy, centralisation, and any form of authoritarian rule. Bose, while initially supportive of democracy, later advocated an authoritarian provisional state for the sake of rapid reconstruction, even naming himself head of state in the Provisional Government of Azad Hind. He sought a synthesis of socialism and fascist discipline, favouring a strong, disciplined state to implement social and economic reforms.  

**Economic policy** – Gandhi promoted a rural, self‑sufficient economy, emphasising handicrafts, manual labour, and the *Nai Talim* system that combined education with vocational training. Bose, on the other hand, pushed for industrialisation and technical education, believing that national reconstruction required science, industry, and the training of students abroad to build new industries upon their return.  

**Social reforms** – Both leaders supported women’s emancipation and education, but their approaches differed. Gandhi advocated free, compulsory education in the vernacular, integrating moral and spiritual lessons, and he opposed the English system. Bose also championed higher education, especially in science and technology, and he supported the abolition of purdah and widow remarriage, but he was more pragmatic in aligning with foreign powers to advance these goals.  

**Ideological stance on internationalism and ideology** – Gandhi was wary of fascism and Nazism, rejecting any alliance with them, whereas Bose admired the discipline of fascist regimes and was willing to collaborate with Germany and Japan, though he maintained a distinct socialist vision that rejected both communism and fascist racial doctrines.  

In sum, Gandhi’s ideology was anchored in non‑violence, decentralised self‑rule, and a moral, village‑based economy, whereas Bose’s ideology embraced militant struggle, a strong central authority, industrial development, and a pragmatic, sometimes authoritarian, approach to achieving independence and national reconstruction.

**Mahatma Gandhi (1869‑1948) vs. Subhas Chandra Bose (1897‑1945)**  
Both men were central figures in the Indian independence movement, but they pursued that goal from very different ideological standpoints. Below is a concise comparison of their core beliefs, strategies, and visions for India.

| Dimension | Mahatma Gandhi | Subhas Chandra Bose |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Fundamental Philosophy** | **Satyagraha (truth‑force) and Ahimsa (non‑violence).** Gandhi believed that moral power derived from personal and collective purity, and that the means used to achieve freedom must be just as pure as the end. | **Armed struggle and revolutionary nationalism.** Bose held that the British could be expelled only through decisive, often violent, action, and that moral purity of means was secondary to the goal of liberation. |
| **View of Violence** | Absolute rejection of violence. Even in the face of repression, Gandhi insisted on non‑violent civil disobedience, fasting, and constructive programmes (e.g., village self‑reliance). | Accepted violence as a legitimate tool. He organized the Indian National Army (INA) and sought alliances with powers (Japan, Germany, Italy) that were themselves engaged in war, believing that “the enemy of my enemy is my friend.” |
| **Political Strategy** | **Mass civil‑disobedience** (e.g., Salt March, Non‑Cooperation Movement, Quit India). Emphasis on non‑cooperation with British institutions, boycotts, and the creation of parallel indigenous institutions (schools, courts, khadi industry). | **Militant organization** (the Forward Bloc within the Congress, later the INA). Sought to raise an army of Indian soldiers to fight the British, and to create a provisional government in exile. |
| **Economic Vision** | **Village‑centrism & Swadeshi.** Gandhi advocated a decentralized, agrarian economy based on self‑sufficient villages, khadi (hand‑spun cloth), and minimal industrialization. He feared large‑scale industry would create new forms of exploitation. | **Industrial and socialist orientation.** Bose supported rapid industrialisation, state‑directed planning, and a mixed economy. He admired aspects of Soviet and German economic models and wanted a strong central state to drive development. |
| **Social Reform** | Integrated social reform with political struggle: eradication of untouchability (Harijan uplift), promotion of women’s education, and communal harmony. He used personal example (simple living, vegetarianism) to inspire moral change. | Also opposed caste discrimination and supported women’s participation, but placed less emphasis on personal moral discipline. His primary focus was on national unity against the British, sometimes downplaying internal social reforms in favour of immediate political goals. |
| **Relationship with the British Empire** | Sought **moral suasion** and a negotiated transfer of power. Gandhi believed that the British could be persuaded to leave if Indians demonstrated moral superiority and self‑governance capacity. | Favoured **direct confrontation**. Bose argued that the British would not relinquish power voluntarily and that only a decisive military defeat could compel withdrawal. |
| **International Outlook** | Preferred **non‑alignment** and moral leadership on the world stage. He was skeptical of aligning with any great power that might compromise India’s ethical stance. | Practised **realpolitik**. He sought support from the Axis powers (Japan, Germany, Italy) because they were enemies of Britain, despite their own authoritarian tendencies. |
| **Vision of Post‑Independence India** | A **spiritual, democratic, decentralized** republic rooted in village self‑rule (Panchayati Raj), with a strong emphasis on moral and ethical development of citizens. | A **strong, centralized, secular** nation-state with a robust bureaucracy and military, capable of rapid industrialisation and able to assert itself as a major global player. |
| **Legacy within the Indian National Congress** | Founder of the Congress’s mass‑based, non‑violent tradition; his methods dominated the party’s mainstream after independence. | Formed the **Forward Bloc** (1939) after breaking with the Congress leadership; his legacy lives on in the reverence for the INA and in the more militant strands of Indian nationalism. |

### Key Points of Divergence

1. **Means vs. Ends**  
   - *Gandhi*: “The means are the end in themselves.” Non‑violence was non‑negotiable.  
   - *Bose*: “The end justifies the means.” Armed struggle was acceptable if it accelerated independence.

2. **Nature of the State**  
   - *Gandhi*: Decentralised, village‑based governance; minimal central authority.  
   - *Bose*: Centralised, strong state apparatus capable of directing economic development and defence.

3. **Economic Policy**  
   - *Gandhi*: Swadeshi, self‑sufficiency, limited industrialisation.  
   - *Bose*: State‑led industrialisation, modern infrastructure, and a mixed economy.

4. **International Alliances**  
   - *Gandhi*: Reluctant to ally with any imperial power; emphasized moral authority.  
   - *Bose*: Pragmatic alliances with the Axis powers to exploit Britain’s wartime vulnerabilities.

5. **Role of Violence**  
   - *Gandhi*: Absolute non‑violence, even in the face of repression.  
   - *Bose*: Embraced armed resistance; organized an army (INA) and sought to fight the British militarily.

### Why the Differences Matter

- **Strategic Impact**: Gandhi’s non‑violent campaigns mobilised millions across social strata, creating a broad-based, morally compelling movement that eventually forced Britain to negotiate. Bose’s militant approach, while never achieving a decisive military victory, inspired a sense of assertive nationalism and demonstrated that Indians could organize a fighting force, influencing post‑independence defence policies.

- **Post‑Independence Policy**: Gandhi’s vision shaped India’s early democratic institutions, emphasis on rural development, and commitment to non‑alignment. Bose’s ideas resurfaced in later debates about industrial policy, central planning, and a more assertive foreign policy (e.g., the Nehru‑vintage “mixed economy” and later the push for a strong defence establishment).

- **Cultural Memory**: Gandhi is globally celebrated as the archetype of non‑violent resistance, while Bose is revered in India as a heroic patriot who dared to take up arms. Both narratives coexist, reflecting the pluralistic nature of Indian nationalism.

### Bottom Line

- **Gandhi** championed a **moral, non‑violent, decentralized** path to freedom, rooted in personal and collective ethical transformation.  
- **Bose** advocated a **militant, centralized, and industrial** approach, willing to use force and strategic alliances to achieve rapid independence.

Understanding these ideological contrasts helps explain the diverse tactics and visions that co‑existed within India’s struggle for freedom and continue to influence its political discourse today.

Agrarian Reforms There were certain basic constraints due to which the Congress ministries could not undertake a complete overhaul of the agrarian structure by completely abolishing zamindari. These constraints were: (i) The ministries did not have adequate powers. (ii) There were inadequate financial resources as a lion’s share was appropriated by the Government of India. (iii) Strategy of class adjustments was another hurdle since zamindars, etc., had to be conciliated and neutralised. (iv) There was constraint of time since the logic of Congress Rule in Provinces ✫✫✫✫✫ 413 Congress politics was confrontation and not cooperation with colonialism. (v) War clouds had started hovering around 1938. (vi) The reactionary second chamber (Legislative Council) dominated by landlords, moneylenders and capitalists in United Provinces, Bihar, Bombay, Madras and Assam had to be conciliated as its support was necessary for legislations. (vii) The agrarian structure was too complex. In spite of these constraints, the Congress ministries managed to legislate a number of laws relating to land reforms, debt relief, forest grazing fee, arrears of rent, land tenures, etc. But most of these benefits went to statutory and occupancy tenants while sub-tenants did not gain much.

During the brief period of Congress rule in the provinces from 1937 to 1939, the ministries introduced a range of reforms aimed at improving the conditions of workers, farmers and civil society.  

**Agrarian reforms** – The Congress governments enacted several laws dealing with land tenure and rural debt.  These included legislation on land reforms, debt relief, forest grazing fees, arrears of rent and the regulation of land tenures.  Although the reforms were limited by constraints such as inadequate powers, financial resources and the need to conciliate landlords, they nevertheless represented a significant step toward restructuring the agrarian system.  

**Civil‑liberty reforms** – The ministries repealed laws that granted emergency powers to the colonial administration and lifted bans on illegal organisations such as the Hindustan Seva Dal and Youth Leagues.  They also removed restrictions on certain books and journals, thereby easing curbs on civil liberties.  

**Labour and trade‑union reforms** – The Congress governments were generally sympathetic to workers’ demands.  The 1937 elections saw the All India Trade Union Congress (AITUC) support Congress candidates, and the ministries gave a “fillip” to trade‑union activity.  Many worker‑friendly legislations were passed during and after the elections, reflecting the ministries’ commitment to labour rights.  

In summary, the Congress ministries introduced agrarian reforms (land, debt, grazing, rent and tenure laws), civil‑liberty reforms (repeal of emergency powers and lifting of bans on organisations and publications), and labour‑friendly legislation that strengthened trade‑union activity and workers’ rights during 1937‑39.

• **Education & Health:** Expansion of rural schools and dispensaries; promotion of Punjabi language in schools. | | **Bengal** (A. K. Ghosh – Premier) | • **Education:** Introduction of free primary education in many districts; emphasis on adult literacy drives.

Simon Commission The 1919 Act had provided that a Royal Commission would be appointed ten years after the Act to report on its working. In November 1927, two years before schedule, the British government announced the appointment of such a commission—the Indian Statutory Commission. The commission submitted its report in 1930. It recommended that dyarchy be abolished, responsible government be extended in the provinces, a federation of British India and the Princely States be established, and that communal electorates be continued. Three Round Table Conferences were called by the British government to consider the proposals. Subsequently, a White Paper on Constitutional Reforms was published by the British government in March 1933 containing provisions for a federal set-up and provincial autonomy. A joint committee of the Houses of the British Parliament was set up under Lord Linlithgow to further consider the scheme. Its report submitted in 1934 said that a federation would be set up if at least 50 per cent of the princely states were ready to join it. The bill prepared on the basis of this report was passed by the British Parliament to become the Government of India Act of 1935. Government of India Act, 1935 ● The Act, with 451 clauses and 15 schedules, contemplated the establishment of an All-India Federation in which Governors’ Provinces and the Chief Commissioners’ Provinces and those Indian states which might accede to be united were to be included. (The ruler of each Princely State willing to join was to sign an ‘instrument of accession’ mentioning the extent to which authority was to be surrendered to the federal government.) ● Dyarchy, rejected by the Simon Commission, was provided for in the Federal Executive. ● The Federal Legislature was to have two chambers (bicameral)—the Council of States and the Federal Legislative 512 ✫✫✫✫✫ A Brief History of Modern India Assembly. The Council of States (the Upper House) was to be a permanent body. ● There was a provision for joint sitting in cases of deadlock between the houses. There were to be three subject- lists—the Federal Legislative List , the Provincial Legislative List and the Concurrent Legislative List . Residuary legislative powers were subject to the discretion of the governor-general. Even if a bill was passed by the federal legislature, the governor-general could veto it, while even Acts assented to by the governor-general could be disallowed by the King-in-Council. ● Dyarchy in the provinces was abolished and provinces were given autonomy, i.e., the distinction between Reserved and Transferred Subjects was abolished and full responsible government was established, subject to certain safeguards. ● Provinces derived their power and authority directly from the British Crown. They were given independent financial powers and resources. Provincial governments could borrow money on their own security. ● Provincial legislatures were further expanded. Bicameral legislatures were provided in the six provinces of Madras, Bombay, Bengal, United Provinces, Bihar and Assam, with other five provinces retaining unicameral legislatures. ● The principles of ‘communal electorates’ and ‘weightage’ were further extended to depressed classes, women and labour.

**Why the Indian National Congress finally entered the 1937 provincial elections under the Government of India Act 1935**

| Factor | What it meant for Congress | How it pushed Congress to contest |
|--------|---------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Realisation that the Act offered genuine provincial autonomy** | The 1935 Act abolished the “diarchy” of the 1919 Act and gave elected ministries almost full control over provincial subjects (education, health, agriculture, local government, etc.). The Governor retained only a few “reserve” powers, but day‑to‑day administration was now in the hands of elected ministers. | Congress could now **exercise power**, test its administrative capacity, and demonstrate that self‑government was feasible. |
| **Political isolation if it stayed out** | By 1935 the **Muslim League** and a host of communal parties were already organising for the elections. If Congress boycotted, the League and other minority groups would dominate provincial legislatures, shaping the political map in ways hostile to a united India. | Participation was seen as the only way to **prevent a Muslim‑League‑led “partition” of power** and to keep the nationalist agenda alive in the legislatures. |
| **Shift in Congress’s strategic doctrine**  | Earlier (1920‑30) Congress had followed a **“non‑cooperation”** line, refusing to legitimize British constitutional reforms. After the failure of the Civil Disobedience Movement (1930‑34) and the disappointment of the Round‑Table Conferences, many leaders concluded that **pure agitation alone could not achieve Swaraj**. | The Working Committee, under Gandhi’s guidance, adopted a **dual strategy**: continue mass agitation while also **entering constitutional politics** to build a parallel power base. |
| **Pressure from the masses and provincial leaders** | Grass‑roots activists, especially in Bengal, Madras, Bombay, United Provinces and the Punjab, were already organising for the elections and demanded a Congress presence. Provincial Congress committees argued that **abstention would betray the people’s aspirations**. | The central leadership relented, allowing provincial units to **contest** while the All‑India Congress kept a “conditional” stance (i.e., they would resign if the Governor misused his reserve powers). |
| **British assurances (though limited)** | The Viceroy, Lord Linlithgow, and the British administration hinted that **legitimate ministries would be allowed to function** and that the Governor would not arbitrarily dismiss them unless “the public interest demanded it.” | These assurances, though vague, gave Congress a **political opening** to test the limits of the new constitutional arrangement. |
| **Desire to expose the flaws of the Act from within** | By taking office, Congress could **highlight the discriminatory aspects** of the Act (separate electorates, the Governor’s veto, the “special” powers over law‑and‑order) and use its platform to demand further reforms. | Contesting the elections became a **tactical move**: win power, then use that power to press for a more democratic constitution. |
| **International context** | The rise of fascism in Europe and the looming threat of another world war made many Indian leaders think that **a united, responsible Indian polity** would be a stronger bargaining chip in any post‑war settlement. | Participation was framed as a way to **show the world that Indians could govern responsibly**, strengthening India’s claim to full independence later. |

### The Decision‑making Process

1. **Early 1935 – Early 1936:**  
   - The Congress Working Committee (CWC) debated the merits of contesting.  
   - Gandhi, while personally skeptical of the Act’s “imperial” features, urged the party to **“test the waters”** and not leave the field to communal parties.

2. **Mid‑1936:**  
   - Provincial Congress committees (e.g., Bombay, Madras, United Provinces) passed resolutions to **contest the elections**.  
   - The CWC adopted a **conditional participation** policy: Congress would contest, form ministries, but **reserve the right to resign** if the Governor’s reserve powers were exercised arbitrarily.

3. **Late 1936 – Early 1937:**  
   - The Congress National Executive formally approved the decision.  
   - A campaign was launched under the slogan **“Swaraj through the ballot box”**, emphasizing that constitutional means complemented mass struggle.

### Outcome

- In the **1937 elections**, Congress won **majorities in 7 of the 11 provinces** (e.g., Madras, Bombay, United Provinces, Bihar, Central Provinces, Orissa, and the North‑Western Frontier Province).  
- The ministries **demonstrated administrative competence**, but soon clashed with the Governor’s reserve powers, leading to the famous **“1939 resignation”** when World War II broke out and the Viceroy asked ministries to support the war effort without consulting them.

### Bottom line

Congress finally agreed to contest the provincial elections because **political pragmatism outweighed ideological purity**. The 1935 Act offered a genuine, though limited, arena for self‑government; staying out would have ceded that arena to communal forces and left the nationalist movement without a foothold in the new constitutional order. By entering the elections, Congress could:

- **Gain experience in governance**,  
- **Counter communal politics**,  
- **Mobilise mass support through a legitimate platform**, and  
- **Use the ministries as a lever to demand further constitutional reforms**.

Thus, participation was a calculated step toward the ultimate goal of **complete independence**.

The growth of communalism in pre‑Independence India was driven by a combination of social, economic, and political factors that emerged with the rise of modernity and colonial rule.  

**Modern ideas and new identities** – The spread of modern concepts of nation, class and cultural‑linguistic identity turned religious consciousness into a communal one.  As new identities were forged in modern economic, political and social institutions, the old pre‑modern identities had not disappeared, creating a clash that produced a communal ideology.  

**Rising middle classes and bourgeois interests** – The middle classes, especially the bourgeois, propagated imaginary communal interests to advance their own economic goals.  Communalism was seen by leftist thinkers as a “bourgeois question” that served the interests of the rising capitalist class.  

**Colonial backing and socio‑economic alignment** – The colonial administration actively supported communalists, using them as a channel to expand its social base.  Socio‑economic distinctions in Indian society often coincided with religious distinctions, so class contradictions were given a post‑facto communal colouring by vested interests.  Conservative reactionary elements also backed communalism.  

**Educational and economic lag of Muslims** – Muslim intellectuals were slower to absorb modern Western thought and scientific ideas, and even after reform movements the proportion of educated Muslims remained far lower than that of Hindus, Parsis or Christians.  Muslims also lagged in trade and industry, leaving a small educated elite that was vulnerable to landlord and zamindar influence.  

**Government employment and special treatment demands** – Educated Muslims, finding few opportunities in business or the professions, turned to government service.  British officials and loyalist Muslim leaders incited educated Muslims against educated Hindus, and reformers such as Syed Ahmad Khan demanded special treatment for Muslims in government service.  This created a sense of fear and a perception that Muslims needed special protection.  

**Use of religion as a vehicle** – In a society with low education and limited awareness of the outside world, religion had the potential to become a vehicle of communalism.  While religiosity itself did not equal communalism, it was used by communalists to mobilise people around imagined communal interests.  

These intertwined causes—modern identity formation, bourgeois economic motives, colonial support, socio‑economic disparities, educational lag, and the strategic use of religion—collectively fostered the growth of communalism in India before independence.
reference:
**Communalism in pre‑Independence India** – i.e., the growing identification of people primarily along religious lines (mainly Hindu‑Muslim, but also Sikh, Christian, etc.) and the political mobilisation that accompanied it – was not the product of a single event. It emerged from a complex interaction of colonial policies, socio‑economic changes, religious reform movements, and political developments. Below is a synthesis of the most widely‑cited causes, grouped thematically and presented chronologically where relevant.

---

## 1. Colonial Administrative Policies

| Policy / Event | How it fostered communal consciousness |
|----------------|------------------------------------------|
| **“Divide‑and‑Rule” strategy** (late 18th‑early 20th c.) | British officials often portrayed Hindus and Muslims as “natural enemies” to justify indirect rule through separate elites. |
| **Census of 1871 onward** | The first systematic classification of the population by religion turned fluid, locally‑defined identities into fixed categories. The census tables were widely publicised, reinforcing the idea of “Hindu” vs. “Muslim” as distinct political communities. |
| **Separate electorates (Indian Councils Act 1909 – “Morley‑Minto Reforms”)** | Muslims were given separate seats in legislative councils, institutionalising the notion that they needed distinct political representation. This created a precedent that communal identity could be a basis for political power. |
| **Communal award of 1932 (Poona Pact & separate electorates for minorities)** | Although the Poona Pact temporarily merged Dalit and Hindu electorates, the broader policy of separate electorates for Muslims, Sikhs, etc., entrenched communal voting patterns. |
| **Land‑revenue and agrarian policies** | The Permanent Settlement (1793) and later revenue reforms created a class of landlords (often Muslim in North India) who were seen as “privileged” by peasant communities, feeding resentment along communal lines. |

---

## 2. Socio‑Economic Transformations

| Change | Communal impact |
|--------|-----------------|
| **Urbanisation & migration** (railways, ports) | Large‑scale movement of people (e.g., Muslim traders to cities, Hindu labourers to plantations) produced new, mixed‑ethnicity neighbourhoods where competition for jobs, housing, and markets could be framed in religious terms. |
| **Industrialisation** (cotton mills, jute, tea) | The emergence of a wage‑labour class created new economic rivalries; employers sometimes pitted Hindu and Muslim workers against each other to prevent labour solidarity. |
| **Commercialisation of agriculture** | In the Punjab and Bengal, cash‑crop cultivation altered traditional patron‑client relations, sometimes aligning them with communal identities (e.g., Muslim landlords vs. Hindu tenants). |
| **Education & the press** | English‑medium schools produced a new elite that could articulate communal grievances in modern political language; newspapers (e.g., *Al-Hilal*, *Kesari*) began publishing communal polemics. |

---

## 3. Religious Reform and Revivalist Movements

| Movement | Effect on communal dynamics |
|----------|-----------------------------|
| **Islamic revivalism** (e.g., *Deoband* (1866), *Aligarh* (1886), *Wahabi* influence) | Emphasised a pan‑Islamic identity, modern legal education, and political consciousness among Muslims, encouraging them to see themselves as a distinct nation‑like community. |
| **Hindu reform movements** (Brahmo Samaj, Arya Samaj, Ramakrishna Mission) | While many sought social renewal, some (especially Arya Samaj) promoted a “pure” Hindu identity and criticised Muslim practices, feeding mutual suspicion. |
| **Sikh resurgence** (Singh Sabha, Gurdwara Reform Movement) | Asserted Sikh distinctiveness vis‑à‑vis both Hindus and Muslims, adding another communal layer. |
| **Christian missionary activity** | Though numerically small, missionary schools and conversion attempts sometimes triggered defensive reactions from Hindu and Muslim groups, reinforcing communal boundaries. |

---

## 4. Political Mobilisation & Leadership

| Development | Communal consequence |
|-------------|----------------------|
| **Formation of the Indian National Congress (1885)** | Initially a cross‑communal platform, but its early leadership was dominated by English‑educated Hindus, leading some Muslims to feel under‑represented. |
| **All‑India Muslim League (1906)** | Created explicitly to safeguard Muslim political interests; its demand for separate electorates and later for “two‑nation” ideas gave an organisational shape to Muslim communal politics. |
| **Hindu Mahasabha (1915) & Rashtriya Swayamsevak Sangh (RSS, 1925)** | Articulated a Hindu nationalist vision, often portraying Muslims as a demographic threat. |
| **Khilafat Movement (1919‑1924)** – a pan‑Islamic protest against the abolition of the Ottoman Caliphate – temporarily united Hindus and Muslims under the Congress banner, but its abrupt end left a vacuum that was later filled by more sectarian politics. |
| **Partition of Bengal (1905) & its reversal (1911)** | The 1905 partition, justified by the British as administrative, was presented by many Muslims as a protective measure and by many Hindus as a “divide‑and‑rule” ploy, sharpening communal awareness. |

---

## 5. Cultural and Ideological Narratives

1. **Historical narratives of conquest and rule** – British school textbooks and popular historiography often highlighted the “Mughal‑Islamic” past as a period of oppression for Hindus, while Muslim historiography emphasized a glorious past of empire. These competing memories fed communal pride and grievance.

2. **Language politics** – The debate over Urdu (associated with Muslims) vs. Hindi (associated with Hindus) in the 1910s‑30s turned linguistic preference into a proxy for religious identity.

3. **Legal codification of personal law** – The British codified separate Hindu and Muslim personal laws (e.g., Hindu Marriage Act 1860, Muslim Personal Law (Shariat) Application Act 1937). By legally recognising distinct family‑law systems, the state reinforced the perception of two separate communities.

---

## 6. External Influences

| Influence | Communal impact |
|-----------|-----------------|
| **World War I & the Khilafat crisis** | Global Muslim solidarity (defence of the Caliphate) resonated in India, encouraging Muslims to see themselves as part of a trans‑national ummah. |
| **Rise of nationalism in other colonies** | The success of nationalist movements elsewhere (e.g., Irish Home Rule) inspired both Hindu and Muslim leaders to adopt more assertive, sometimes exclusive, political strategies. |
| **International Islamic organisations** (e.g., the *Jamiat‑Ulema‑e‑Hind*) – though largely anti‑colonial – provided ideological links to broader Muslim political thought. |

---

## 7. Cumulative Effect: From “Communalism” to “Communal Politics”

- **From social identity to political demand**: Initially, religious identity was a cultural marker. Over the 19th and early 20th centuries, it became a *political* claim‑making tool, especially after the British institutionalised separate electorates.
- **From elite discourse to mass mobilisation**: Early communal debates were confined to scholars and aristocrats. By the 1920s‑30s, mass parties (Muslim League, Hindu Mahasabha, RSS) were able to mobilise large sections of the population through rallies, newspapers, and later, cinema.
- **From occasional riots to systematic competition**: Communal riots (e.g., 1857, 1908, 1919) were episodic, but by the 1930s they increasingly coincided with elections, indicating that communal sentiment had become a strategic electoral weapon.

---

## 8. Summary – Key Interlocking Causes

| Category | Core Mechanism |
|----------|----------------|
| **Colonial governance** | Institutionalisation of communal categories (census, separate electorates) and “divide‑and‑rule” tactics. |
| **Economic change** | Competition for jobs, land, and markets in a rapidly modernising economy, often framed in religious terms. |
| **Religious revivalism** | Reform movements that re‑asserted distinct religious identities and histories. |
| **Political organisation** | Creation of communal parties (Muslim League, Hindu Mahasabha, RSS) that gave a political voice to communal grievances. |
| **Cultural narratives** | Textbooks, press, and public discourse that highlighted historical antagonisms and celebrated separate traditions. |
| **External events** | Global Islamic politics, World War I, and the broader anti‑colonial wave that encouraged communal solidarity as a form of resistance. |

These factors did not act in isolation; they reinforced each other. For instance, the British census gave a statistical basis to the communal narratives promoted by religious reformers, which in turn were harnessed by political parties to demand separate representation. The cumulative outcome was a deepening of communal consciousness that, by the 1940s, made the prospect of a united, secular India increasingly contested and ultimately led to the Partition of 1947.

The four proposals – the August Offer, the Cripps Mission, the Wavell Plan and the Cabinet Mission Plan – were all attempts by the British to resolve India’s constitutional future, but they differed markedly in their timing, content and reception.

**August Offer (1940)** – Presented by Viceroy Lord Linlithgow, it was a response to the changing fortunes of the war. It promised dominion status for India, an expanded executive council with a majority of Indians, a post‑war constituent assembly largely composed of Indians, and a requirement that any future constitution obtain the consent of minorities. The Congress rejected it, calling the dominion idea “dead” and seeing it as widening the gulf with Britain. The Muslim League welcomed the offer, especially the veto assurance, and used it to reinforce its demand for partition.

**Cripps Mission (1942)** – The mission’s plan also offered dominion status and a constituent assembly, but it made it clear that the British Crown would not transfer its paramountcy to any other party. The Congress rejected the plan, largely because it did not address the issue of princely states and the extent of Indian autonomy.

**Wavell Plan (1945)** – Wavell’s “Breakdown Plan” was a middle‑ground proposal that envisaged the withdrawal of British forces and officials from the Muslim provinces of North‑West and North‑East, leaving the rest of the country under Congress control. It did not provide for elected representatives from princely states in the constituent assembly and was seen by some as an attempt to create a “Northern Ireland”‑style Pakistan. The plan was not accepted by either major party.

**Cabinet Mission Plan (1946)** – The Cabinet Mission’s proposal rejected the idea of a fully independent Pakistan, arguing that such a state would contain large non‑Muslim populations and would create severe economic, administrative and military problems. It grouped the existing provincial assemblies into three sections (A – Hindu‑majority provinces; B – Muslim‑majority Punjab, NWFP and Sindh; C – Muslim‑majority Bengal and Assam) and called for an interim government and a constitution that would be framed by a constituent assembly. The plan was an attempt to keep India united while addressing communal concerns, but it ultimately failed to bring the Congress and the Muslim League into agreement.

In summary, the August Offer and the Cripps Mission both sought dominion status but were rejected by the Congress; the Wavell Plan attempted a partial transfer of power to the Congress but lacked provisions for princely states; and the Cabinet Mission Plan offered a more detailed grouping of provinces and a rejection of full partition, yet it could not reconcile the divergent demands of the Congress and the Muslim League.

Below is a side‑by‑side comparison of the four major British initiatives that tried to resolve the constitutional dead‑lock in India during the Second World War and its immediate aftermath. | **Feature** | **August Offer (1940)** | **Cripps Mission (1942)** | **Wavell Plan (1945)** | **Cabinet Mission Plan (1946)** | |-------------|--------------------------|---------------------------|------------------------|--------------------------------| | **Date & Context** | 8 August 1940 – Britain at war, seeking Indian support for the war effort. | 22 March 1942 – After the fall of Singapore & Japanese threat; Britain needed Indian manpower and resources. | 14 January 1945 – War in Europe almost over; Britain wanted a post‑war settlement and to restore civil administration. | 3 March 1946 – War over, communal violence rising; Britain wanted a united, independent India (or at least a peaceful partition). | | **Primary Objective** | Secure Indian cooperation in the war by offering limited constitutional concessions. | Obtain Indian agreement to support the war in exchange for a promise of dominion status after the war. | Replace the Viceroy’s Executive Council with an Indian‑led council and set the stage for a new constitution. | Draft a constitution for a united India (or a federation) that would lead to independence by June 1948. | | **Key Provisions** | • Expansion of the Viceroy’s Executive Council (increase Indian members).
• No separate electorates; a *“common franchise”* for all communities (though not implemented).
• Assurance that the *“future constitution”* would be framed after the war. | • Offer of **Dominion Status** after the war (full internal self‑government).
• Creation of a **Constituent Assembly** to frame a constitution (members to be elected by provincial legislatures).
• Provision for **minority safeguards** (e.g., separate electorates could continue). | • Re‑constitution of the Executive Council with **Indian majority** (7 Indians, 3 British).
• Transfer of **all powers of the Viceroy** (except defence, foreign affairs) to the council.
• Formation of a **Constituent Assembly** to be elected by provincial legislatures (no direct elections). | • **Three‑tier federal structure**: a central government, groups of provinces, and princely states.
• **Two options** for provinces: (a) join a united India, or (b) form a separate group (later interpreted as “Pakistan”).
• **Constituent Assembly** to be elected by provincial legislatures (no direct elections).
• Provision for **princely states** to join either group or remain independent. | | **Who Was Involved?** | Viceroy Lord Linlithgow and Indian leaders (Congress, Muslim League, others). | Sir **Sir Stafford Cripps** (British envoy) and Indian leaders (Congress, Muslim League, Sikh leaders, princely states). | Field Marshal **Lord Wavell** (Viceroy) and Indian political parties (Congress, Muslim League, others). | **Cabinet Mission** – three British ministers (Lord Pethick‑Lawrence, Sir Stafford Cripps, A.V. Alexander) plus Indian leaders (Congress, Muslim League, princely states). | | **Indian Reaction** | • **Congress**: Rejected – felt the offer was too vague, no real power transfer, and the “common franchise” was unrealistic.
• **Muslim League**: Skeptical, demanded separate electorates. | • **Congress**: Refused to accept without a clear guarantee of immediate self‑government; demanded *“complete independence”* (Purna Swaraj).
• **Muslim League**: Also rejected, fearing domination by Congress. | • **Congress**: Accepted the *“Indian majority”* in the council but demanded a **larger role** and a **clear timeline** for a constituent assembly.
• **Muslim League**: Accepted the plan as a step forward but remained wary of Congress dominance. | • **Congress**: Accepted the plan (with the “*simultaneous*” acceptance clause) but later withdrew over the *“grouping of provinces”* clause.
• **Muslim League**: Accepted initially, then demanded a *“single, undivided Pakistan”* and rejected the grouping option. | | **Outcome / Impact** | – No substantive change in power.
– Indian political parties continued the **Quit India Movement** (1942). | – Mission failed; both Congress and the League rejected it.
– Led to **“Quit India”** (August 1942) and a harsher British crackdown. | – The plan was **never implemented** because the British government in London rejected it; it was superseded by the Cabinet Mission. | – The plan **initially succeeded** in forming a Constituent Assembly (elected in 1946).
– Disagreements over *grouping* and *partition* led to the **breakdown of negotiations**, culminating in the **partition of India** (August 1947). | | **Significance in the Road to Independence** | First formal British concession after the 1935 Act, but too limited to satisfy Indian aspirations. | Marked the first explicit promise of **Dominion status** (later seen as a stepping stone to full independence). | Showed Britain’s willingness to give Indians a larger share in governance, but the plan’s failure highlighted the need for a more comprehensive settlement. | The **most detailed** and *practically executed* proposal; its failure directly precipitated the **partition** and the creation of **India and Pakistan** in 1947. | | **Key Similarities** | • All were **British‑initiated** attempts to secure Indian cooperation during/after WWII.
• Each promised a **future Constituent Assembly** or constitutional reform.
• All faced **rejection or partial acceptance** because they did not meet the demand for *complete* self‑government. | • Same as above. | • Same as above. | • Same as above. | | **Key Differences** | • Offered **no concrete timeline**; vague “after the war” clause.
• No mention of a Constituent Assembly. | • Explicit promise of **Dominion status** *after* the war.
• Proposed a **single‑chamber Constituent Assembly** elected by provincial legislatures. | • Focused on **executive power** (re‑constituting the council) rather than a full constitution.
• Did not address the *future* constitution in detail. | • Provided a **detailed federal structure** with options for *grouping* provinces.
• Addressed the **princely states** explicitly.
• Set a **deadline** (June 1948) for independence. | --- ### Narrative Summary 1. **August Offer (1940)** – A wartime overture that tried to placate Indian opinion by modestly increasing Indian representation in the Viceroy’s Executive Council and promising a post‑war constitution. It lacked any concrete timetable or guarantee of real power, so both the Indian National Congress and the Muslim League dismissed it. 2. **Cripps Mission (1942)** – Sir Stafford Cripps came with a more generous promise: India would become a **Dominion** after the war, and a **Constituent Assembly** would be elected to draft a constitution. The Congress wanted *immediate* independence; the League feared a Hindu‑dominated Dominion. The mission collapsed, leading to the **Quit India Movement** and a harsher British clamp‑down. 3. **Wavell Plan (1945)** – With the war winding down, Viceroy Lord Wavell tried to give Indians a larger share of executive power by creating an Indian‑majority Executive Council and again proposing a Constituent Assembly elected by provincial legislatures. The plan was never put into effect because the British government in London rejected it, but it signalled a shift toward a more Indian‑led administration. 4. **Cabinet Mission Plan (1946)** – The most comprehensive of all, the three‑minister Cabinet Mission offered a **federal constitution** with a strong centre, grouped provinces (which later became the basis for the “two‑nation” demand), and a clear path to independence by June 1948. Initially both the Congress and the League accepted, but disagreements over the *grouping* clause and the League’s insistence on a separate Pakistan caused the plan to unravel, leading directly to the **partition** of the sub‑continent in August 1947. --- ### Bottom Line - **All four proposals** were British attempts to keep India within the Empire while extracting wartime support, and each promised a future constitutional settlement. - **Progressive escalation:** The August Offer was the most modest; the Cripps Mission added a Dominion promise; the Wavell Plan shifted power to an Indian‑led executive; the Cabinet Mission gave a detailed federal blueprint and a concrete deadline. - **Why they failed:** None satisfied the **dual demand** of the Indian National Congress (full, immediate independence) and the Muslim League (adequate safeguards against Hindu majoritarianism). The failure of the first three left the Cabinet Mission as the last serious effort, and its collapse precipitated the **partition** that ultimately ended British rule.

National Army The idea of creating an army out of the Indian prisoners of war (POWs) was originally that of Mohan Singh, an Indian army officer who had decided not to join the retreating 458 ✫✫✫✫✫ A Brief History of Modern India British army in Malaya. He decided to turn to the Japanese for help. The Japanese had till then encouraged Indian civilians to form anti-British organisations. Mohan Singh asked for Indian prisoners of war. The Japanese handed over the Indian prisoners of war to Mohan Singh who tried to recruit them into an Indian National Army. After the fall of Singapore, several POWs were ready to join Mohan Singh. By the end of 1942, 40,000 men were ready to join the INA. It was intended that the INA would go into action only on the invitation of the Indian National Congress and the people of India. The move to form this army has been seen by many as a check against the misconduct of the Japanese against Indians in South-East Asia and as a bulwark against a possible future Japanese occupation of India. The INA got a boost with the outbreak of the Quit India Movement in India. In September 1942, the first division of the INA was formed with 16,300 men. With the Japanese contemplating an Indian invasion, the idea of an armed wing of INA seemed more relevant to them. But soon, serious differences emerged between the Indian Army officers led by Mohan Singh and the Japanese over the role to be played by the INA. Actually, the Japanese wanted a token force of 2,000 only while Mohan Singh wanted to raise a much larger army. Mohan Singh was taken into custoday by the Japanese. The second phase began with the arrival of Subhash Bose in Singapore. But before that in June 1943, Subhash Chandra Bose (under pseudo name Abid Hussain) reached Tokyo; met the Japanese prime minister, Tojo. The role of Rasbehari Bose, another great freedom fighter, should also be acknowledged here. He had fled to Japan in 1915 following the failed revolutionary activities. In Japan, Rashbehari Bose eventually became a naturalised citizen. He made a lot of effort in getting the Japanese interested in the Indian independence movement. He became active in Pan-Asian circles, founded the Indian Club of Tokyo, and gave lectures on the evils of Western imperialism. Very early itself he was impressed by Subhash Chandra Bose. When the Indian National Army was formed by Mohan Singh in Singapore, Rashbehari Bose was greatly excited and Quit India Movement, Demand for Pakistan, ... ✫✫✫✫✫ 459 left Tokyo for Southeast Asia. It was at a conference in Bangkok (also under Japanese occupation at the time) that it was decided to place the INA under an Indian Independence League whose chairman would be Rashbehari Bose himself. He had created the League in 1942 in Tokyo. When Subhash Bose was sought by the Japanese to lead the INA, he was ready for it. He went to Singapore and met Rashbehari Bose, and the latter happily transferred the control and leadership of the Indian Independence League and the INA to Subhash in July 1943. It must be noted that it was on the organisational spadework done by Rashbehari Bose that Subhas Bose could build up the Indian National Army. Subhash Bose became Supreme Commander of the INA on August 25. (In February 1944, after a collapse of the lungs, Rashbehari’s health steadily deteriorated, and he died on January 21, 1945, aged 58.) On October 21, 1943, Subhash Bose formed the Provisional Government for Free India at Singapore with H.C. Chatterjee (Finance portfolio), M.A.

The Indian National Army (INA) did not win a decisive military victory against the British, but its very existence and activities had a lasting impact on India’s freedom struggle.  

**Symbolic power and morale** – The INA was formed from Indian prisoners of war who were recruited by Mohan Singh and later led by Subhas Chandra Bose.  By the end of 1942 it had 40,000 men ready to fight, and in September 1942 a first division of 16,300 men was raised.  The fact that Indians could raise a sizeable army, even under Japanese auspices, gave the Indian population a tangible example of armed resistance and boosted the morale of the Quit India movement, which had begun the same year.  

**Political influence on the Congress and the national movement** – The INA’s creation was intended to act only on the invitation of the Indian National Congress and the people of India.  Its existence forced the Congress to confront the reality that “it was no longer possible to rule India without the wishes of Indians.”  The movement’s success in placing the demand for independence on the immediate agenda of the national movement is attributed in part to the INA’s example of armed struggle.  

**International dimension and pressure on Britain** – The INA was formed with Japanese support, and its establishment was seen as a check against Japanese misconduct and a bulwark against future occupation.  The INA’s presence on the battlefield, even though it was ultimately defeated, added a new international dimension to the Indian struggle and increased pressure on Britain, especially as the Allies (USA, USSR, China) were already urging Britain to grant substantial power to Indians.  

**Legacy and long‑term impact** – After its defeat, the INA’s legacy lived on in the form of heightened nationalism, the erosion of loyalty to the colonial administration, and the demonstration that a united, armed Indian force could challenge British rule.  The movement’s spontaneous and popular character, combined with the leadership of figures such as Subhas Bose and Rashbehari Bose, left an indelible mark on the freedom struggle, inspiring future generations of freedom fighters and contributing to the eventual attainment of independence.  

In short, the INA’s influence lay not in battlefield victories but in its symbolic power, its ability to galvanise public sentiment, its role in reshaping the political strategy of the Congress, and its contribution to the broader narrative that India could not be governed without the consent of its people.
reference:
**The Indian National Army (INA) – a brief overview**

| Aspect | Details |
|--------|---------|
| **Founding** | Formed in 1942 under Japanese patronage from Indian prisoners of war (POWs) captured in Malaya, Singapore and later from Indian expatriates in Southeast Asia. |
| **Leadership** | Initially led by **Captain Mohan Singh**, later taken over by **Subhas Chandra Bose** (aka Netaji) in 1943, who gave the force its most recognizable shape and vision. |
| **Goal** | To liberate India from British rule by force, aligning with the Axis powers only as a means to that end. |
| **Size & Operations** | At its peak, ~40,000–45,000 men. Fought alongside Japanese forces in the Burma campaign (Imphal, Kohima, and the advance toward the Indian frontier) in 1944. |
| **Outcome** | Militarily defeated; the Japanese surrender in 1945 ended the INA’s campaign. Many soldiers were captured, and the army dissolved. |

---

## 1. Why the INA mattered beyond battlefield results

| Influence | How it manifested |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Symbolic “armed” challenge to British rule** | The INA was the first organized Indian force that *explicitly* declared war against the British Crown. Its existence proved that a segment of Indians was willing to take up arms, countering the British narrative that Indians were content under colonial rule. |
| **Mobilisation of Indian diaspora and prisoners of war** | Tens of thousands of Indian soldiers who had fought for the British were convinced to switch sides. This demonstrated that loyalty to the British was not monolithic, shaking the confidence of the colonial administration in the reliability of its own Indian troops. |
| **Psychological impact on the British Indian Army** | After the INA’s formation, the British began to fear mutinies. The *“Bengal Mutiny”* of 1946 (the Royal Indian Navy mutiny) and the *“Tiranga”* protests among army units were partly inspired by the INA’s example. |
| **Catalyst for political realignment** | The INA forced mainstream Indian political parties—especially the Indian National Congress and the Muslim League—to confront the question of armed struggle versus non‑violent resistance, broadening the strategic discourse of the freedom movement. |
| **Creation of a martyr‑mythology** | The trials of INA officers (the *Red Fort Trials* of 1945‑46) turned the soldiers into national heroes. Their courtroom speeches, especially those of **Shahnawaz Khan**, **Prem Kumar Sahgal**, and **General Shah Nawaz**, were broadcast widely, stirring public sympathy. |
| **Boost to popular nationalism** | Songs like *“Kadam Kadam Badaye Ja”* and *“Subhas Chandra Bose”* anthems, as well as the iconic slogan “**Give me blood, and I shall give you freedom**,” entered the popular imagination, especially among youth and the working class. |
| **International attention** | The INA’s alliance with Japan and its presence in Southeast Asia forced the British to defend their empire on a global stage, exposing the contradictions of a “white‑dominant” empire fighting a war of liberation against a colonised people. |
| **Post‑war political leverage** | After the war, many former INA members entered mainstream politics (e.g., **Lakshmi Sahgal**, **B. G. Kher**, **Mohan Singh**, **Shah Nawaz Khan**). Their presence in the political arena added pressure on the British to negotiate a quicker transfer of power. |

---

## 2. The Red Fort Trials – The turning point

1. **Publicity** – The British decided to try 13 INA officers for *waging war against the King* and *treason*. The trials were held at the historic Red Fort in Delhi, a potent symbol of Indian sovereignty.
2. **Massive public mobilisation** – Across India, massive rallies, petitions, and newspaper campaigns demanded the release of the accused. Even the Indian National Congress, which had previously condemned the INA’s alliance with the Axis, joined the “**INA Defence Committee**.”
3. **Political fallout** – The trials exposed the British to a wave of criticism: “How can a colonial power prosecute soldiers who fought for their own country’s freedom?” This eroded the moral high ground the British claimed.
4. **Outcome** – Although most were sentenced to life imprisonment, the British eventually commuted many sentences under pressure. The trials had already achieved a political victory: they turned the INA into a **national cause célèbre** and forced the British to reckon with a new, more militant public sentiment.

---

## 3. Influence on specific events and movements

| Event / Movement | INA’s Influence |
|------------------|-----------------|
| **Royal Indian Navy Mutiny (Feb 1946)** | The mutineers quoted INA slogans and demanded the release of INA prisoners. The mutiny’s rapid spread (over 20,000 sailors) showed the potency of the INA’s inspirational legacy. |
| **Quit India Movement (1942)** | Although the Quit India movement preceded the INA’s formation, the INA’s later emergence reinforced the idea that *armed* resistance could complement *non‑violent* civil disobedience, keeping the momentum alive after the British cracked down on the 1942 protests. |
| **Partition debates** | The INA’s pan‑Indian, secular rhetoric (Bose’s vision of a united, independent India) added a counter‑narrative to communal politics, influencing leaders like **Jawaharlal Nehru** to stress a united front against colonialism. |
| **Post‑war elections (1945‑46)** | Former INA members contested elections, and the *Congress* used the INA’s popularity to pressure the British for a swift transfer of power, arguing that the British could no longer claim the loyalty of Indian soldiers. |
| **Formation of the Indian National Army Veterans Association (INA‑VAA)** | After independence, the veterans lobbied for recognition, pensions, and the right to bear arms. Their activism kept the INA’s legacy alive in the early republic, influencing defence policy debates. |

---

## 4. Long‑term cultural and ideological legacy

1. **National heroes and icons** – Subhas Chandra Bose, Lakshmi Sahgal, and other INA leaders are commemorated on Indian stamps, coins, and in school curricula. Their stories are taught as examples of *courage* and *self‑sacrifice*.
2. **Popular culture** – Films like *“Netaji Subhas Chandra Bose: The Forgotten Hero”* (2005), *“The Forgotten Army – Azaadi Ke Liye”* (2020, Netflix series), and numerous books keep the INA narrative alive for new generations.
3. **Military ethos** – The Indian Armed Forces honour the INA’s contribution through regimental histories and memorials (e.g., the *INA Memorial* at the Red Fort). The idea that *“the nation’s defence is a duty of every citizen”* echoes the INA’s recruitment drive.
4. **Political symbolism** – Various political parties invoke the INA to claim a legacy of *strong nationalism* (e.g., the Bharatiya Janata Party’s references to “Netaji’s vision”) while left‑leaning groups highlight Bose’s socialist inclinations.
5. **Diaspora memory** – In Malaysia, Singapore, and Myanmar, INA veterans formed community organisations that helped preserve the memory of the struggle and contributed to post‑colonial nation‑building narratives in those countries as well.

---

## 5. Summarising the INA’s impact despite defeat

| Dimension | Key Take‑aways |
|-----------|----------------|
| **Military** | Failed to capture Indian territory; suffered heavy casualties; the campaign ended with Japan’s surrender. |
| **Political** | Forced the British to confront the reality that a sizable segment of Indian soldiers and civilians were willing to fight the Crown; accelerated negotiations for independence. |
| **Social** | Created a powerful martyr‑myth that united diverse sections of Indian society (students, workers, soldiers, diaspora). |
| **Legal/International** | The Red Fort Trials turned a legal proceeding into a massive political rally, exposing the limits of colonial law. |
| **Ideological** | Demonstrated that *armed struggle* could coexist with *mass civil disobedience*, broadening the strategic toolbox of the freedom movement. |
| **Cultural** | Cemented a narrative of *heroic sacrifice* that continues to shape Indian national identity and political discourse. |

**Bottom line:**  
Even though the INA never succeeded on the battlefield, its *symbolic power* far outlasted its military defeat. By challenging the British claim to Indian loyalty, inspiring mass protests, and turning its leaders into national martyrs, the INA added a decisive, militant dimension to the Indian freedom struggle—one that hastened the end of colonial rule and left an indelible imprint on India’s post‑independence identity.
